sentence: Subjective:

I reviewed the patient's history and complaints, which include persistent asthma symptoms, specifically coughing and wheezing, occurring almost daily, with exacerbations occurring every month or every other month for the past six months.
The patient has had multiple visits to the urgent care and primary care doctor for these symptoms, and has been prescribed antibiotics, steroids, and inhalers.
The patient has a history of requiring hospitalization for asthma symptoms at a young age, but not since then.
The patient has also mentioned that their symptoms are triggered by getting sick with a cold and by being around their aunt's cat.
Past medical history:
The patient has asthma, for which they have been prescribed albuterol (ventolin) 2 puffs every 4-6 hours as needed, and has a history of hospitalization for asthma symptoms at a young age.
Review of systems:
The patient denies any history of seasonal allergies or other respiratory symptoms.
Current medications:
I prescribed albuterol (ventolin) 2 puffs every 4-6 hours as needed for asthma symptoms.
Objective:
Vital signs:
Blood pressure: 128/82
Respiratory rate: 16
Oxygen saturation: 99% on room air
Physical exam:
The patient's lung exam revealed diminished lung sounds throughout with occasional slight expiratory wheeze, bilaterally.
The neck exam was supple, trachea was midline, and no lymphadenopathy was appreciated.
The carotid bruit was not appreciated, and the patient had strong bilateral pulses with no clubbing on their fingertips and brisk capillary refill.
Test Results:
The patient's pulmonary function test results are consistent with asthma.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has moderate intermittent asthma.
Problem list: Asthma; active.
Plan:
I started the patient on flovent 110 micrograms, 1 puff twice per day, to help prevent daily symptoms and reduce the need for albuterol.
The patient can continue to use their albuterol inhaler as needed.
I will put together an asthma action plan to help manage symptoms and exacerbations.
The patient should continue to use their albuterol inhaler 1-2 puffs and wait to see how their breathing is before taking additional doses.
Follow-up:
I will see the patient in 30 days or sooner if they experience any worsening symptoms.
